evals.prompt: I have an async FastAPI application using SQLAlchemy 2.0 with asyncpg. I need to load a User along with their Team and all the team's Projects in a single endpoint. The User has a many-to-one relationship to Team, and Team has a one-to-many relationship to Project. Show me how to set up the async session, write the query with proper eager loading, and handle the case where the user has no team. I keep getting MissingGreenlet errors.
evals.expected_output: A complete solution showing async session configuration with expire_on_commit=False, a select() query using joinedload for the User->Team relationship and selectinload for Team->Projects, proper error handling for None team, and explanation of why MissingGreenlet occurs.
assertions: Output uses expire_on_commit=False in async_sessionmaker configuration to prevent MissingGreenlet errors after commit
Query uses joinedload for the many-to-one User.team relationship (single LEFT JOIN, efficient for single objects)
Query uses selectinload for the one-to-many Team.projects collection (separate IN query, avoids cartesian product)
Solution includes handling for user.team being None before accessing team.projects
Code uses SQLAlchemy 2.0 select() syntax instead of legacy Query API

evals.prompt: I need to add a non-nullable 'role' enum column (admin, member, viewer) to our existing 'users' table that has 2 million rows in production Postgres. Write the Alembic migration that won't lock the table or fail on existing rows. Also show me how to add a concurrent index on the new column. We use async SQLAlchemy with asyncpg.
evals.expected_output: A multi-step Alembic migration that adds the column safely: first as nullable with server_default, then optionally alters to non-nullable, with concurrent index creation outside a transaction, and both upgrade() and downgrade() functions.
assertions: Migration adds the column with server_default to handle existing rows without failure on the 2M row table
Migration uses postgresql_concurrently=True or equivalent for index creation to avoid locking the table
Migration includes both upgrade() and downgrade() functions for rollback capability
Column type uses a Python enum mapped to database ENUM or VARCHAR with CHECK constraint
Migration handles the transaction boundary correctly for concurrent index creation (commits or runs outside transaction)
